data.title: Telos: Rapid Growth Surge And Still Under-Recognized In The Market
data.description:
We're about to head into the Q4 earnings season, and the stock market remains incredibly volatile. Investors continue to bear major concerns about frothy valuations in the wake of potential macroeconomic disruption. And though no massive rotation has happened yet, my core strategy for managing a potential downturn is to bias my portfolio toward small- and mid-cap stocks, particularly those that trade at great value despite having appealing growth catalysts to spark a rebound.

Telos (TLS) is a stock that few investors have probably heard of, and yet through its supplier relationship with TSA PreCheck, it's a company that many U.S. travelers have likely interacted with. Primarily a government software provider, Telos has had a breakout year over the past year, rising ~80% and dramatically beating the S&P 500 - and with a small market cap still under $500 million and modest multiples, Telos still has a long way to go.

When it comes to my specific investing strategy for this year, Telos checks off many boxes. The company is off recent highs (as are most software stocks), giving us a decent entry point. Fundamentally, the company is showcasing rapid demand growth as it releases new products, diversifying its revenue base; and on top of this, the company has a healthy balance sheet with no debt. I'm initiating this stock at a "Buy" rating.

Cybersecurity and identity platform, with key government clients

First, let's get a solid grounding on what exactly Telos does. Telos is admittedly old in software company years: it was founded 57 years ago in 1969, and its original products were centered around secure network administration. Today, however, the company has broadened. Its two main products, detailed in the chart below, are called Xacta.Ai and Telos ID.

Xacta.ai falls into a realm of cybersecurity called "GRC" (governance, risk, and compliance). Its main use case is to automate the process of detecting cyber risk within a company's network. The company notes that it takes only 9 days to implement Xacta technology into a company's existing tech stack (versus months for outdated manual processes), and within that time, the analytics offered by Xacta can help IT leaders identify red flags and gaps that need to be addressed.

Telos ID, meanwhile, is the main source of the company's recent growth spurt. The dominant client of Telos ID is TSA PreCheck, in which the government recently boosted spending for security, which flowed nicely into Telos' revenue stream. Telos ID is the platform that handles TSA PreCheck enrollment on behalf of the federal government, and the most recent year specifically benefited from the conversion into using Real ID across all states.

On the enterprise side, Telos ID sells a product called IDTrust360. The platform helps organizations with identity scanning and matching, offering fingerprint identification and facial recognition, pulling up criminal histories, and enabling access and granting appointments.

Together, Xacta and Telos ID form the company's growing "Security Solutions" segment. The company still operates its legacy segment, "Secure Networks," which helps with endpoint security to enable remote work logins and to help secure traditional enterprise networks. However, this business will gradually sunset over time as the company focuses on its new cybersecurity and government-based offerings.

Rapid recent growth, driven by Telos ID

Though Telos is far from a household name, 2025 (and the government's investment into TSA PreCheck) has made Telos one of the hottest growth stocks in the cybersecurity space. The chart below showcases Telos' most recent quarterly results:

Telos' revenue in Q3 grew at an unheard-of 116% y/y pace to $51.4 million, with the Security Solutions segment growing 154% y/y driven by the outperformance of Telos ID (PreCheck) and the legacy network segment declining 9% y/y. We note that Telos remains at a small scale, what I consider to be a "starter company" that is on the path to much broader monetization.

The core growth driver here is the company's expansion of its TSA PreCheck enrollment centers. It had originally marched toward a target of operating over 500 enrollment locations by the end of 2025; the company is tracking well ahead of that target with 504 locations in Q3. We do note that Telos doesn't have exclusivity here: TSA PreCheck has three authorized enrollment partners, alongside IDEMIA and Clear Secure (YOU), with the latter probably the best-known among the three due to the fast-lane Clear gates that stand out at most U.S. airports.

The company isn't expecting growth to slow down anytime soon. Its Q4 outlook calls for 67%-76% y/y growth, as shown in the chart below:

We note as well that Telos' new products are also enabling the company to achieve significant profit maturation. At this point last year, Telos was still a relatively small-scale software vendor with negative adjusted EBITDA. Now, the company is targeting a ~10% adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year, allowing us to fully justify the company's valuation from a bottom-line standpoint.

As we look ahead to next year, Telos has also already outlined that its pre-existing programs and products are expected to generate at least $180 million in revenue. This suggests $17 million of incremental revenue (~10% y/y growth) on top of this year's $162-$164 million full-year guide, without any contribution from new products and customers factored in. Meanwhile, the company notes that its pipeline entering 2026 is healthy. On the Q&A portion of the Q3 earnings call, CEO John Wood noted as follows:

Pipeline into '26 is still substantial. There's a lot of opportunity in there. It's still a multibillion-dollar pipeline, with a good chunk of that expected to be awarded, say, over the next 6 months with several tens of millions of dollars of revenue opportunity in 2026 on top of the $180 million from pre-existing programs.

We note there are also healthy signs of revenue diversification beyond government deals, with the company recently announcing in January that it signed a $5.4 million GRC cybersecurity deal with a large technology company.

Valuation still leaves room for upside

Despite Telos' sharp rally over the past year, the stock still trades at quite modest valuation multiples. At current share prices in the low $5s, Telos trades at a market cap of $410.8 million. Netting off the $59.1 million of cash (against zero debt) on Telos' latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $351.7 million.

For the current year FY26, Wall Street is currently expecting Telos to generate $195.3 million in revenue. If we conservatively assume that Telos can achieve ~1 point of y/y margin expansion in FY26, or a 10.6% adjusted EBITDA margin versus a 9.6% guidance midpoint in FY25, adjusted EBITDA against this revenue profile would be $20.7 million. This positions Telos' valuation multiples at:

  • 1.8x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 17.0x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

Needless to say, Telos is a unique business with few direct comps, other than the other publicly traded TSA enrollment provider: Clear. Telos trades at lower revenue multiples than Clear, but a ~50% richer adjusted EBITDA profile.

That said, I think this premium is more than justified. Telos is still growing from a small scale, whereas Clear is a much more mature company with an annualized revenue run rate of nearly $1 billion (~4-5x Telos' current scale). Also, we think Clear's ~30% adjusted EBITDA margin provides a solid roadmap for the level of profitability that Telos can achieve when it scales to become closer to Clear's level.

Risks, Q4 look-ahead, and key takeaways

Of course, we should also be cognizant of the risks that Telos faces. Competition is a major one: as previously mentioned, Clear is both much larger and better recognized than Telos. In the cybersecurity space, the company faces plenty of GRC competition from the likes of SAP (SAP), AuditBoard, and ServiceNow's GRC product.

We note as well that much of Telos' growth is centered on the TSA PreCheck expansion. While the federal government is certainly a reliable partner in providing recurring revenue, we note that Telos will need to diversify its revenue streams to truly grow into Clear's scale.

That said, with modest multiples and a long runway ahead, Telos remains a great under-recognized small-cap "Buy." The next major catalyst for Telos is its Q4 earnings release, expected in mid-February. Here, what we're mainly looking for is the company's FY26 outlook. We expect management to flesh out more details between its previous statement of generating $180 million in revenue from existing products, and what growth opportunities it sees on top. We think this is highly likely to be a positive catalyst for the company that will extend the recent rally, and recommend buying ahead of earnings.

data.title: CMS Rates Proposal Shatters Health Insurance Stocks: What Investors Must Know (& Do)
data.description:
Investment Overview

Yesterday, US health insurance stocks took an almighty beating, as news broke that the Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services ("CMS") had announced a proposed rate increase for Medicare Advantage ("MA") Capitation Rates and MA and Part D Payment Policies for the calendar year ("CY") 2027.

According to CMS' statement:

If finalized, the proposed policies are projected to result in a net average year-over-year payment increase of 0.09%, or over $700 million in MA payments to plans in CY 2027.

For 2026, the CMS hiked rates by 5.06%, or >$25bn in additional payments, and for 2025, the rate was 3.7%, or ~$16bn. When we consider the fact that the 2025 rate rise was greeted with widespread consternation by all major US health insurers, and resulted in a slew of missed earnings, revised (downward) guidance, and devastating share price losses, we can begin to understand the reaction to yesterday's news.

If 3.7% was regarded as shockingly low, health insurance CEO's must have thought it was April Fool's Day when they checked their inboxes yesterday, and saw a figure of 0.09% for 2027.

Medicare Advantage ("MA") healthcare plans are for Americans aged over 65, paid for by the U.S. government, and administered by private health insurers - as I wrote in a note covering UnitedHealth (UNH) and Humana (HUM) yesterday:

According to KFF.org, an independent research organization, ~54% of eligible Medicare beneficiaries are enrolled in MA plans - ~34.1m people out of 62.8m eligible. That's up from ~19% in 2007. The share is forecast to increase to ~64% by 2034, and ~70% of all enrollees have independent plans, which are open for general enrollment.

UnitedHealth has 29% of all MA enrollees - ~9.9m members, with Humana second, 17%, or 5.7m enrollees, and CVS third with 12%, or ~4.1m members. Centene has ~1m members.

As mentioned, I discussed the impact on UnitedHealth and Humana, who have the largest exposure to MA in the note shared yesterday; however, by no means have the share prices of other insurers with lesser exposure escaped heavy punishment, as we can see in the table below:

The table covers most of the major US health insurers, plus a few smaller companies, and some with exposure to MA. As we can see, every company's stock lost value yesterday, the average being -12%, while over a three-year period, Alignment Healthcare (ALHC) and Oscar Health (OSCR) aside, returns have been extremely poor.

Seeking Alpha has asked me to pick over the bones of yesterday's events to see if, among the general malaise, there may be opportunities for investors to "buy the dip", "buy low and sell high", take a "contrarian" position, or uncover an opportunity the market may be missing, so let's start our analysis by looking at the biggest insurers - UNH and HUM aside, and seeing if we can find some positives.

CVS, Elevance, Cigna, and Centene - Searching For Good News Stories

If we begin with CVS Health (CVS), this is the company with the third highest number of MA members, thanks to its pivot into health insurance via its ~$78bn acquisition of Aetna in 2018. According to the company's investor day presentation from December last year, CVS has 2.9m individual members, and 1.3m group members.

On a 1-year basis, CVS stock is actually up 48%, although this is largely due to its stocks' torrid performance in 2023 and 2024, as its massive bet on the MA industry began to unravel. Shares fell from >$100 in November 2022, to $44 in December 2024 - their lowest value in over a decade - as the company missed earnings targets, blaming higher healthcare utilization by MA plan members - as per CVS' 2024 annual report / 10K filing:

Medical benefit ratio is calculated as health care costs divided by premium revenues and represents the percentage of premium revenues spent on medical benefits for the Company’s Insured members.

The MBR increased from 86.2% to 92.5% in 2024 compared to the prior year primarily driven by increased utilization, the unfavorable impact of the Company’s Medicare Advantage star ratings for the 2024 payment year and higher acuity in Medicaid following the resumption of redeterminations.

The higher the MBR, the less profitable the MA business is, however in 2025, with some help from the activist investor Glenview, CVS decreased its MBR from 95.2% in Q3 2024, to 92.8%. The MBR also decreased to 90.0% in the nine months ended September 30, 2025 compared to 91.7% in the prior year.

CVS outlines a target margin from its MA business of 3% - 4% in its investor day presentation; however, this target likely assumes that the CMS would hike payment rates for 2027 by a widely expected 4% - 6%, not by <0.1%, hence the sell-off on CVS stock yesterday.

CVS, of course, has other businesses - 2.3m Medicaid, and 18m commercial insurance members, plus a pharmacy benefit management ("PBM") business, CVS Caremark, and its 9k retail locations, and 1k walk-in clinics.

The issue here is that PBM's are also in the Trump Administration's crosshairs, with the President determined to closely regulate how these agencies determine drug prices, and lower them, and regarding retail, the fates of Walgreens Boots Alliance and Rite Aid illustrate the challenge CVS faces extracting value from this business.

CVS has invested >$20bn in two businesses, home healthcare specialist Signify Health and physician's network Oak Street Health to try to pivot the health insurance industry from Fee For Service ("FFS"), to "Value Based Care" - feels paid out according to patient outcomes - but it seems clear that CVS and the US government are not in step on policy, and my guess is CVS will soon be forced to downgrade its forecast for mid-teens CAGR earnings per share ("EPS") growth through 2028, and may well look to exit more and more territories where it is currently offering MA plans.

Elevance, Centene - Major Insurers Struggling To Make MA - Or ACA - Work

How about Elevance (ELV)? This is an insurer with ~3.12m MA members, out of 45.3m total medical membership (source: Q3 10Q filing). Today, the company has announced its 2025 earnings - $197.6bn of revenues, diluted EPS of $25.21, adjusted EPS of $30.29, and also provided guidance for 2026.

Unfortunately, projected EPS for 2026 is down significantly on 2025 - $22.3 on a diluted basis, and $25.5 on an adjusted basis, and the company noted that:

The benefit expense ratio was 93.5 percent in the fourth quarter (of 2025), an increase of 110 basis points compared to the prior year period, reflecting higher medical cost trend primarily in our Affordable Care Act health plans and heightened Medicare Part D seasonality driven by Inflation Reduction Act changes.

Furthermore, during a presentation given at the UBS Global Healthcare Conference in December, Elevance's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") told the audience:

So for 2026, we've approached our Medicare Advantage bids with a focus on financial discipline and long-term sustainability. We undertook a comprehensive review of our offerings, and we made the strategic decision to exit select plans and service areas where the economics no longer support our return objectives. And we expect these changes to impact approximately 150,000 members.

Elevance, like another major health insurer, Centene (CNC), also has heavy exposure to the Affordable Care Act ("ACA") marketplace, however - you may have guessed it by now - this is also an industry in turmoil.

In July last year, Centene was forced to withdraw its 2025 guidance, owing to higher-than-expected medical costs, as well as "rising morbidity" in its ACA and Medicaid populations.

In the ACA market, the company is reliant on ACA subsidies, introduced as part of Obamacare; however, the Trump administration may allow these subsidies to expire, which could lead to higher uninsured rates and higher per-member costs after 2026.

In short, then, in Elevance and Centene, you have two more companies whose core businesses face significant headwinds in 2026, and beyond, with no obvious solutions - other than a reversal of government policy regarding MA, and ACA.

Smaller Names Struggling To Take Advantage Of Incumbents' Struggles

Oscar Health (OSCR) is a company I hold stock in, yet despite some recent strong gains - from >$2.5 per share in early 2023, to >$20 in September last year, this is another company with heavy exposure to the ACA marketplace, currently characterized by uncertainty, as the market waits to see if the government will extend the subsidies discussed above.

As Oscar's CFO put it during a discussion at the UBS Healthcare conference in December:

We do think there's a portion of the market that will not be able to afford the premium and will likely leave the marketplace next year. And our estimate for the marketplace is that it will contract by 20% to 30% next year. And most of that is on the back of individuals that can no longer afford the premium and will leave the marketplace. And that assumes that enhanced subsidies did -- were allowed to sunset and expired.

Of course, if subsidies are extended, it would be great for Oscar and its business, and its share price, and that remains a possibility. Meanwhile, the insurer claims that "we think we've got great opportunities even if the subsidies don't get extended".

Alignment Healthcare (ALHC) is another name that a contrarian investor might consider, after its shares fell in value by ~12% yesterday. Speaking at the JP Morgan Healthcare conference this month, Alignment's Founder, CEO & Director John Kao told the audience:

Over the last 10 years or so, we have grown tremendously. We have grown responsibly. We are over 275,000 members now. And we kind of guided to close to 300,000 for the end of the year. We're approaching on a guidance basis, $4 billion in premium revenue. We've basically been growing at 30% per year. We've effectively been doubling every 2, 2.5 years. So I think the vision, the mission of the company is starting to be proved out from a financial perspective.

The CEO also stated:

the vision is to make sure that the health plans, the providers, hospitals and doctors, the brokers and very, very importantly, CMS are all working together in an aligned way with data fluidity, aligned economic incentives, operational seamlessness, all kind of functioning together for the benefit of the senior.

CEO Kao also criticized three "levers" of the health insurance industry that "are no longer going to be viable moving forward" - namely, coding arbitrage (reclassifying risk codes linked to higher payments), global capitation, and denial and denial rates.

At least the CEO seems to be on the same wavelength as the government and the public in terms of wanting to make health insurance less about perceived profiteering and more about putting the patient first, and the company is guiding for 27% membership growth in 2026.

Could an investment ahead of the company's Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings announcement around February 26th pay-off?

Unfortunately, my suspicion is that yesterday's news is just as bad for Alignment as it is for the larger companies as regards 2026 performance; however, I would categorize Alignment and Oscar as, potentially, in a position to capitalize on the current industry turmoil in the longer-term, given they are more agile, smaller companies, designed for the digital age of healthcare.

A final cautious recommendation from me would be Cigna (CI) - the company exited its MA business entirely last year, and outlined some radical new plans for its PBM business during its presentation at the UBS conference last November.

The company pays a generous dividend presently yielding ~2.2%, trades at low P/E and P/S ratios, and its shares were less affected yesterday than any other insurer, as its core focus is now elsewhere. Granted, President Trump is no admirer of the PBM industry, but Cigna may be enacting the kind of wholesale changes the President is demanding just in time to reap the short and long-term benefits of being aligned with government, as opposed to at loggerheads with it.

Concluding Thoughts - Fear, Uncertainty & Doubt Abounds - It Takes A Brave Investor To Bet On A Health Insurance Sector Recovery

If ever a policy decision sent a strong message that government and industry were poles apart in their understanding of a business landscape, the CMS' rate rise announced yesterday would surely be it.

The figure of 0.09% is almost provocative, in my view, and also arguably suggests that the recent Capitol Hill grilling of senior health insurer execs appears to have been a catastrophic failure for the industry.

It's clear that the White House and the health insurance industry are not remotely aligned on policy, either in the Medicaid, Medicare, marketplace, or ACA of PBM sectors, and so far, two of its largest incumbents, UnitedHealth and Elevance, have posted shockingly disappointing guidance.

Unfortunately, while the government insists health insurers waste money or pay themselves too much money, and offers them only the most negligible of MA rate hikes, insurers will likely respond by suggesting it is the government who are hanging on to money they shouldn't be (to fund tax cuts or to invest elsewhere, perhaps?), and stating they have no choice but to pass costs onto plan members.

Alternatively, insurers could simply stop offering plans in unprofitable territories, or patients could stop buying health insurance if costs and premiums keep rising.

In summary, at present, it's hard to escape the feeling that the health insurance sector will be stuck in a rut for some considerable time, until some common ground can be found, and it's hard to know when that may be.

The Government, with its recent launch of "The Great Healthcare Plan", is clearly not backing down, although we may make the point that governments come and go every four years, while businesses endure.

There is also one important thing to remember about the rates proposal that may give a brave contrarian investor or a long-term insurance sector investor hope, however. Yesterday's rate proposal is not final, with a final rate announcement due on or before April 6th.

Given that the current Trump Administration seems to have an appetite for bringing businesses to the negotiating table and striking deals, it's possible the final rate will be revised upwards - but by >4% - 5%, in line with initial expectations? I doubt that is likely, and as such, looking for value within this industry today, I'd very much favor the younger, smaller, more agile insurers - Oscar and Alignment - over the beleaguered incumbents, and based on its stated willingness to adapt, Cigna also.

Does that mean you should "panic sell" your holdings in CVS, Elevance, or Centene today? Absolutely not, in my view, as there will eventually be some clarity around what a consensus driven future for the industry might look like, and it's hard to imagine it could be worse than things look today.

data.title: Cheniere Energy: Strong Cash Flow, Long-Term Contracts, And AI Tailwinds
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Cheniere Energy (LNG), I highlighted their strong cash flow and solid projects that are advancing, backed by long-term tailwinds supporting the demand for LNG despite near-term pressure.

After a solid report and improved guidance despite operational challenges, Cheniere remains a Buy in my opinion, offering a solid way to gain exposure to the future of LNG through a fundamentally solid company that should be able to withstand the geopolitical uncertainty faced by their industry.

Internal Developments

Cheniere reported a solid Q3, with their projects advancing well while also upgrading their FY 2025 guidance, now estimating a distributable cash flow between $4.8 billion and $5.2 billion, well above the previous $4.4 billion to $4.8 billion, while the company expects to export a record >50 million metric tons of LNG for the first time in 2026 following the Corpus Christi expansion.

Financially, based on LNG’s latest report, we continue to see an overall good position, with the current assets almost covering the current liabilities and a manageable amount of debt, especially once their CAPEX normalizes and they reap the benefits of their ramped-up investments.

They also repurchased ~4.4 million shares for ~$1.0 billion in Q3, bringing the 9M’25 total to $1.69 billion worth of stock, annualizing to about $2.25, for an annualized yield of ~5%, which is certainly not bad. As a note, they have about $2.2 billion left under the current authorization as of the Q3 report.

Combined with the ~1.07% dividend representing a cost of about $450 million, this overall solid yield represents a very sustainable payout ratio based on their DCF, while they continue to focus on their expansion, investing ~$2 billion in growth CAPEX and repaying ~$352 million in long-term debt during the first 9 months of 2025.

LNG’s Q4 Earnings report should be released on February 26th, with Normalized EPS expected to reach $3.87 and $5.48 billion in revenue, and I’m looking forward to more guidance for 2026 since this is the Q4 report and insights into the current macro situation, especially as fear around Greenland still persists.

Macro Developments

To begin with, the worries around Greenland that were somewhat relieved following a deal framework made the EU quickly pause Trump’s $750 billion LNG agreement (part of the $1.5 trillion broader trade package), which is currently still on pause, potentially hurting the prospects of the company’s investments even though the EU should continue to be a significant buyer even if we see the war in Ukraine reach a resolution.

As a note, the demand for LNG will also depend on the economy, and higher costs of capital and tariff uncertainty combined with the risk of seeing fewer investments in the sector as the market is fearing Big Tech’s “overspending” on AI can certainly pressure prices, at least in the near term.

In fact, the fear around Big Tech’s AI/Cloud CAPEX isn’t new, and although it was reignited in recent months, it was also a reason why many of these stocks fell significantly a couple of years ago when announcing their ramped-up plans. I’ll mention again that I believe the companies that are so deep in the field and basically gave us the “definition” of what we now call AI likely know better than the general market what’s needed going forward, and there’s likely a good reason why everyone is rushing to build the infrastructure now.

At the same time, S&P Global recently highlighted the significant disparity between the CAPEX of American companies compared to Chinese tech giants, which could ultimately lead to more demand for natural gas or LNG from China as they boosted CAPEX a lot but still have plenty of room to catch up. This can unlock even more demand on a global scale, potentially boosting LNG/gas prices throughout the cycle.

Although far from a big threat now, Canada is also advancing in an interesting direction following the change in administration, with the “Grand Bargain” potentially helping their oil and gas industry in the long term while boosting their access to the West. Meanwhile, their recent trade agreement with China prompted Trump to threaten 100% tariffs, while the country is trying to adapt to “new global realities,” which is a trend highlighted by a recent survey that shows China could be the big winner of President Trump’s policies.

At worst, not only can this lead to more gas and LNG production in Canada in the long-term or even data centers being built there for better economics (which is also mentioned in the MOU), but it can also change the dynamics of their local gas markets, potentially giving Canadian gas producers (including some that sell to Cheniere, such as Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), which signed a 15-year agreement with them back in 2025, starting in 2030) more leverage, as the local AECO prices could rise from their current multi-decade slump - although that seems like a potential issue many years into the future, as the Grand Bargain for example is just a memorandum of understanding, for now.

However, over the long term, I continue to be very bullish on the commodity, with massive potential even in a shifting geopolitical landscape, as AI is expected to require massive amounts of energy. Goldman Sachs recently forecasted that the global power demand from data centers could rise 165% by 2030 compared to 2023, estimating a CAGR ranging from 14% to 20% from 2025 to 2028, while companies and even states invest a lot in the infrastructure behind it.

At the same time, the Electric Power Research Institute estimates that data centers could consume up to 9% of US electricity generation per year by 2030 (more than doubling compared to 4.4% of the total electricity demand in 2023), while the US Department of Energy had an even more aggressive forecast of up to 12% in 2028.

Cheniere was also upgraded at Wolfe recently, with the analyst sharing similar views to mine and rating them an Outperform with a $220 price target, citing the company's solid valuation despite ongoing fears of an “overbuilt” LNG market, saying that a 14% free cash flow yield in 2028-29 upon the Corpus 8 and 9 startup is the highest in the industry, while the company remains 90% contracted under fixed-fee arrangements for an average of 16 years.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, although the company is very solid and has significant long-term potential, they will remain a commodity producer, which can imply significant volatility and dependence on external market factors.

This pressure can result in more delays, including for Cheniere’s projects, as they can decide to pause some stages of development to wait for better commodity prices, which would have direct effects on their profits and the market’s expectations/valuation. These kinds of delays aren’t really uncommon, and we’ve seen BHP (BHP), for example, do it with their massive potash project in Canada about half a year ago.

Valuation

For Cheniere’s DCF to equity valuation, I’ll start with the lowest point of the new guidance of $4.8 billion in DCF in 2025 as a base, followed by a CAGR of 4% over the first 5 years (below even the consensus revenue estimates) to take into account potential pressure on the market, offset by their buybacks lowering the number of shares (effect is visible in the final per share amount), debt repayments and potential from their new construction and CAPEX normalizing, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate despite the significant long-term potential, with a high 12% discount rate to take into account the volatility and risks tied to the industry, and 215 million shares outstanding as of October 24, 2025.

This way, we get to an equity value of $56.57 billion, for an intrinsic value of $263.13 per share, which is still above the current $206.51, leading to a solid margin of safety.

Conclusions

I reiterate Cheniere Energy’s Buy rating, with the current price still reflecting a significant discount to their fair value, offering solid exposure to long-term tailwinds while the market is fearing near-term issues.

There are certainly risks, and volatility is a given, especially as we’re talking about a commodity company that can prove to be very volatile, but Cheniere seems to be in an overall strong position to capitalize on the potential long-term trends and their energy demand.

data.title: Tilray Has Already Done The Hard Part
data.description:
Introduction

Even a cursory glance at the industry will reveal that cannabis stocks have been taking a beating over the last few years. Legalization, once hailed as the ‘Green Rush,’ and fierce competition have eaten into margins and profitability. However, there are a few bright spots on the horizon, mainly linked to a renewed push towards federal reclassification in the USA. There are a few reasons to be bullish on Tilray (TLRY) if this does indeed occur. The company is positioned to benefit disproportionately if U.S. legalization arrives while it remains solvent, diversified, and operationally ready.

Tilray’s Business is Increasingly International

The company is increasingly pivoting towards international distribution. Although Canadian cannabis remained a large contributor to revenue last year, the international segment grew significantly. For FY 2025, the company posted revenue growth of 19% in this segment, having redirected some of its domestic stock towards export.

This trend has continued into fiscal 2026. For Q2 2026, the company reported a 36% increase in international cannabis sales versus a 6% increase in Canadian adult-use revenue. Although the company beat on revenue with a record quarter, margins paint a more nuanced picture. Both gross profit and gross margin contracted, but thanks to strong volume, the overall net loss narrowed from $85.3 million to $41.8 million year over year.

Europe as a Proving Ground

Tilray’s European operations contribute most of its growth in the international segment, with Germany leading the way following its legalization in 2024. Its distribution platform in Germany serves some 13,000 pharmacies through Tilray Pharma. From its European hub in Portugal, Tilray exports to some 20 countries.

With the launch of Tilray Medical Italia, the company is consolidating and rebranding its Italian operations while expanding its portfolio of EU-GMP certified medical cannabis products approved by the Italian Ministry of Health. Distribution is supported by a partnership with Molteni Farmaceutici, providing access to hospitals and pharmacies nationwide. The move reinforces Tilray’s strategy of scaling international medical cannabis through regulated channels, emphasizing margin quality, physician access, and repeat prescription demand rather than volume-led recreational growth, as reported by Cannabis Business Times.

Aside from contributing significantly to revenue, Tilray’s success in Europe has taught it how to navigate a tightly regulated industry in developed foreign markets. Although Europe can’t be compared to the United States due to its fragmentation, vast cultural and linguistic differences, and uneven moves towards legalization, it has proven to be a fertile proving ground.

The Road to Washington

By far the biggest news story in the cannabis industry over the past year is the plan to reschedule cannabis in the United States at a federal level. This would move cannabis from a Schedule I to a Schedule III, essentially reclassifying it as a less dangerous drug. Although this does not constitute federal legalization, it does reduce a number of important hurdles for cannabis companies, including tax and research restrictions. Importantly, this move would not open up the USA as an export market for Tilray. Instead, it signals increasing political momentum towards legalization.

Fortunately for Tilray, it already has a way into the USA through its beverage operations. Tilray Beverages is the fourth largest producer of craft beer in the United States. This provides the company with national distribution relationships, retail access, logistics, and consumer branding infrastructure that it could quickly put to use once legislation allows for things like cannabis-infused beverages.

What It Would Take for Tilray to Be Profitable

Tilray’s path to profitability does not require a dramatic recovery in Canadian cannabis pricing or a sudden explosion in U.S. demand. First, international medical cannabis needs to continue growing at a double-digit pace. Recent results suggest this is already happening, driven primarily by Germany and other European markets where medical access is expanding under regulated frameworks. These markets tend to offer more stable pricing and higher margin potential than Canadian adult-use cannabis.

Secondly, Tilray is no longer struggling to survive. Stabilization in the domestic market means that its core business is intact. It now has some breathing room to grow on its own terms. For Tilray, this means aggressively targeting foreign markets and establishing both infrastructural and regulatory routes. Its success in Europe means it already knows how to do this.

What completes the profitability bridge is what happens below EBITDA. For Tilray Brands, the primary drag has not been gross margin collapse but interest expense and restructuring runoff following several years of consolidation. As those costs normalize, incremental EBITDA has a clearer path to converting into cash flow. The key inflection is not rapid growth but EBITDA consistently covering interest and maintenance capital expenditure, which reduces dilution pressure and balance-sheet risk.

Valuation Shows Skepticism, Not Collapse

On valuation, Tilray looks inexpensive relative to what it already produces rather than what it might become. At roughly 1.2x EV to sales and around 0.6x price to book, the market is valuing the company as if its asset base and revenue mix have limited long-term utility. That is a harsh assumption for a business that has already absorbed multiple rounds of write-downs, moved back to a net cash position, and now generates positive adjusted EBITDA. This is not a deep value stock on earnings, but it is inexpensive on assets and revenue, particularly for a company that has diversified away from a single troubled market. If EBITDA stabilizes and book value proves durable, valuation support does not require heroic assumptions.

Conclusion

Tilray is increasingly a diversified platform with international medical exposure, U.S. consumer infrastructure, and a balance sheet designed to survive prolonged regulatory delay. The company’s path to profitability runs less through near-term cannabis pricing and more through Washington’s slow but consequential movement toward reform. Rescheduling alone does not unlock the U.S. market for Tilray, but it reinforces the strategic logic behind the company’s positioning. If federal legalization or a regulated medical framework ultimately follows, Tilray is positioned to participate without having to rebuild its operating model. If reform takes longer, the company’s diversification reduces the risk of permanent impairment.

data.title: 3 U.S. Tech Stocks To Watch After Nasdaq 100 Bullish Breakout Above 25,830 (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The price actions of the Nasdaq 100 CFD index (a proxy of the Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures) have staged the expected bullish breakout above the former minor 7-week range resistance of 25,830 and rallied by almost 1% on Tuesday, and it has continued to extend its gains on Wednesday, January 28 Asian session with a rally of 0.6% to record an intraday level of 26,170 at the time of writing.

The year-to-date laggard among the major US stock indices has started to catch up. So far, the week-to-date performance as of 26 January 2026, the cash market Nasdaq 100 has recorded a gain of 1.3%, surpassing the S&P 500 (+0.9%), Dow Jones Industrial Average (-0.2%), and the small-cap Russell 2000 (-0.1%).

Amazon, Cisco Systems, Meta Platforms may outperform Nasdaq 100

This report will highlight three US technology stocks (Amazon (AMZN), Cisco Systems (CSCO) and Meta Platforms (META)) that may outperform the Nasdaq 100 (in terms of positive alpha generation) filter by technical factors and volatility-adjusted relative strength (VARS).

In a quick note, VARS is an indicator that normalises momentum by accounting for a stock's volatility, helping traders identify genuine market strength and avoid misleading signals from high-beta, high-volatility shares.

Read more about this guide on volatility-adjusted relative strength: VARS: How to prevent the high-beta trap and find the relevant stocks

Here are the technical charts of the three US technology stocks that may generate positive alpha over the medium-term horizon (1 to 3 weeks).

Amazon (AMZN) bullish reintegration back above 20-day & 50-day MA

Fig. 1: Amazon medium-term trend as of Jan 17, 2026 (Source: TradingView)

Amazon will report its Q4 2025 earnings on Thursday, February 5, after the close of the US session.

In the past three trading sessions, the share price of Amazon has rebounded and traded back above its 20-day and 50-day moving averages.

The volatility-adjusted relative strength of Amazon against the Nasdaq 100 exchange-traded fund has started to slope upwards significantly since December 3, 2025, and traded above its 50-day moving average as well as above its zero line since December 26, 2025 (see Fig. 1).

This observation on VARS suggests that Amazon has started to outperform the Nasdaq 100, a transition from its multi-month underperformance that took place from April 15, 2025 to October 17, 2025.

In addition, its daily RSI momentum indicator is also evolving in a bullish momentum condition as it managed to stage a rebound from its ascending support and crossed above the 50 level, while still below its overbought region (above the 70 level).

Watch the 226.50 key medium-term pivotal support to maintain the bullish bias for the next medium-term resistances to come in at 258.60 (current all-time high) and 275.26 next in the first step.

However, failure to hold at 226.50 and a daily close below it will jeopardize the bulls to trigger a multi-week corrective decline to expose the next medium-term supports at 211.40 and 194.70 next.

Cisco Systems (CSCO) bullish momentum breakout

Fig. 2: Cisco Systems medium-term trend as of Jan 27, 2026 (Source: TradingView)

Cisco Systems will report its Q4 2025 earnings on Wednesday, February 18, 2026, after the close of the US session.

The share price of Cisco has traded back above its 20-day and 50-day moving averages.

The daily RSI momentum indicator has just staged a bullish breakout from its prior descending resistance and rebounded back above the 50 level on January 26, 2026.

In addition, the volatility-adjusted relative strength of Cisco against the Nasdaq 100 exchange-traded fund has started to slope upwards significantly since October 29, 2025 and traded above its 50-day moving average as well as above its zero line since December 29, 2035.

This observation on VARS suggests that Cisco has started to outperform the Nasdaq 100, a transition from its multi-month underperformance that took place from May 27, 2025 to September 22, 2025.

Watch the 72.80 key medium-term pivotal support to maintain the bullish bias for the next medium-term resistances to come in at 82.00 (current all-time high), 85.62, and 90.79/91.54 next in the first step (see Fig. 2).

On the other hand, failure to hold at 72.80 and a daily close below invalidates the bullish scenario to open scope for a multi-week corrective decline to expose the next medium-term supports at 68.89 and 66.50 next.

Meta Platforms (META)'s prior 27% plunge halted at long-term ascending channel support

Fig. 3: Meta Platforms medium-term trend as of Jan 27, 2026 (Source: TradingView)

Meta Platforms will report its Q4 2025 earnings on Wednesday, January 28, 2026, after the close of the US session.

The 27% decline seen in Meta Platforms from its current all-time high of 796.25 printed on August 15, 2025 to the November 19, 2025 low of 581.25 has managed to pause right at the long-term secular ascending channel support in place since the October 2022 low.

Several positive technical elements have emerged. The price actions of Meta Platforms have formed a “higher low” on January 20, 2025, rallied by around 9.8%, and closed above its 20-day and 50-day moving averages (see Fig. 3).

The daily RSI momentum indicator has staged a bullish breakout above its descending resistance and the 50 level, which suggests medium-term bullish momentum may have resurfaced.

The volatility-adjusted relative strength (VARS) of Meta Platforms against the S&P 500 exchange-traded fund (SPY) has started to turn up (shaped a “higher low”) above zero and traded back up above its 50-day moving average. These observations suggest the relative weakness of Meta Platforms may be starting to gain back some strength.

Watch the 585.77 key medium-term pivotal support, and a clearance above 705.20 intermediate resistance may see the medium-term resistances coming in at 758.40 and 793.70 (current all-time high area).

On the flip side, a break and a daily close below 585.77 invalidates the bullish recovery scenario to expose the next medium-term supports at 516.50 and 479.80.
